concerns.text: FR-003 requires comparing σ_SI to 'Xenon1T [deferred] CL limit'. The term '[deferred]' is a placeholder for a value, not a verifiable constraint source. A functional requirement cannot be checked if the reference limit is undefined. The spec must specify the exact data source (URL/DOI) or the interpolation method to be used for the limit curve, as the Constitution (Principle VII) prohibits using placeholders for constraint data.

FR-005 lists 'σ_SI ≤ Xenon1T [deferred] CL limit' as a pass/fail condition. Without a defined limit value or curve, this condition is untestable. The system cannot be verified to 'satisfy' a constraint that is explicitly marked as missing/deferred in the requirement itself.

SC-001 and US-1 require the script to complete in '≤ 30 minutes' on a '2-core CPU'. While the time is measurable, the '2-core CPU' environment is ambiguous in a cloud runner context (e.g., GitHub Actions 'ubuntu-latest' varies). The spec must define the specific runner type or CPU architecture to make the latency threshold reproducible and verifiable.

FR-002 mandates 'numerical integration of the Boltzmann equation including Sommerfeld enhancement'. This is a complex numerical procedure. The requirement is not verifiable without specifying the tolerance, integration method (e.g., Runge-Kutta order), or a benchmark against which the numerical result is validated. 'Numerical integration' alone is not a deterministic testable condition.

FR-003 requires calculating 'loop-induced spin-independent scattering cross-section σ_SI (via photon penguins)'. For a leptophilic model, the conversion from lepton-loop to nucleon-level cross-section (hadronic matrix elements) is non-trivial and physically undefined in the spec. Without specifying the conversion formula or approximation used, the calculated σ_SI cannot be verified against the Xenon1T limit.

US-2 requires validating Δa_μ against 'benchmark values published in the 2014... paper'. The spec does not list the specific benchmark parameter set or the expected numerical result in the acceptance criteria. While the text mentions a point in US-2-1, the requirement FR-001/US-2 lacks a definitive 'expected value' table or explicit reference to the exact benchmark numbers, making the '≤ 2% relative error' check ambiguous if the reference value is not explicitly stated in the spec.
